revision.comment: Bot: mark 2 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24122753975|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[en:Atami Onsen]][[ja:熱海温泉]][[ko:아타미 온천]][[zh:熱海溫泉]] {{Otheruseslist|{{ill|Atami Onsen in Shizuoka|ja|静岡県の熱海温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Onsen in Shizuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502389|1=Atami Onsen in Shizuoka}}|{{ill|Atami Onsen in Fukushima|ja|福島県の磐梯熱海温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Onsen in Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502391|1=Atami Onsen in Fukushima}}|磐梯熱海温泉|{{ill|Atami Onsen in Oita|ja|大分県のあたみ温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Onsen in Oita|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502394|1=Atami Onsen in Oita}}|あたみ温泉|qq=}} '''Atami Onsen''' (熱海温泉【あたみおんせん】) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}’s {{ill|Atami|en|Atami|qid=Q653364|lt=Atami City|1=Atami}}. It is reputed to be one of the {{ill|Three Great Hot Springs|ja|日本三大一覧#三大温泉|lt=Three Great Onsen|lt_ja=三大温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Hot Springs|1=Japan's Top 3|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10311536}} of Japan. == Water Quality == * Sodium–Calcium Chloride Spring – 80% * Sodium–Calcium Chloride–Sulfate Spring – 19% * Sodium–Calcium Sulfate Spring – 1% ** 18,000 liters per minute discharge rate ** A colorless, transparent {{ill|source|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/source|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} ** A total of 410 sources (over 600 including those from Izu-yama and Amishiro) ** A high-temperature spring with a discharge temperature of 98.2°C The majority of the sources are {{ill|Brine spring|en|Brine spring|qid=Q11428612|lt=Brine spring|1=Brine spring}}. Along the mountainsides, many sources are {{ill|Sulfate Springs|ja|硫酸塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate Springs|1=Sulfate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11587674}}. In ancient times, most sources were of the sulfate type. However, due to extensive source development by {{ill|Boring (earth)|en|Boring (earth)|qid=Q12177444|lt=Borehole drilling|1=Boring (earth)}}, an increased amount of {{ill|Seawater|en|Seawater|qid=Q184395|lt=seawater|1=Seawater}} mixed into the underground veins led to a change in the {{ill|water quality|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|1=Hot Spring Types|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/water quality|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}}. In the past, sources located near the {{ill|Tokaido Main Line|ja|Tōkaidō Main Line|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokaido Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502397|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} tracks were of the chloride–sulfate type, but since the later Shōwa period many have shifted to being predominantly chloride springs. Compared with neutral chloride springs in other prefectures, the higher sulfate content makes the water slightly alkaline, resulting in a smoother feel on the skin. == Hot Spring Town == [[File:Atami Iyeyasu hot spring.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ie-yasu no Yu|ja|家康の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ie-yasu no Yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493085|1=Atami Iyeyasu hot spring}}]] [[File:Atami 01.jpg|thumb|Souvenir shops in the hot spring town]] [[File:Great Fire of Atami.JPG|thumb|Thumbnail|The Atami Ginza Shopping Street burned down in the 1950 {{ill|Atami Great Fire|ja|熱海大火|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Great Fire|1=Atami Great Fire|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131919958}}]] At the northeastern tip of the {{ill|Izu Peninsula|en|Izu Peninsula|qid=Q822822|lt=Izu Peninsula|1=Izu Peninsula}}, along the seacoast facing the {{ill|Sagami-nada|ja|相模灘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sagami-nada|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 相模湾|1=Sagami Bay|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1061221}}, from the northeast to the southeast of {{ill|Atami Station|en|Atami Station|qid=Q800412|lt=Atami Station|1=Atami Station}}, a row of ryokan and hotels stretches along the coast. Facilities seeking scenic views are also built on the mountainside. The traditional {{ill|Spa town|en|Spa town|qid=Q4946461|lt=Onsen town|1=Spa town}} has long spread from the station area at the foot of the mountain to the seacoast. The tourist area includes the shopping complex {{ill|Lasca Atami|ja|ラスカ熱海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lasca Atami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熱海駅|1=Atami Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q800412}}, the shopping street near the station, attractions such as {{ill|Kiyunkaku|ja|起雲閣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyunkaku|1=Kiunkaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11636134}}, seashore bathing facilities, and further to the south, locations such as {{ill|Nishikigaura|ja|錦ヶ浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikigaura|1=Nishikigaura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11650415}}, {{ill|Atami Castle|en|Atami Castle|qid=Q11257168|lt=Atami Castle|1=Atami Castle}}, and even the island of {{ill|Hatsushima|en|Hatsushima|qid=Q689643|lt=Hatsushima|1=Hatsushima}}, making it a comprehensive tourist destination. {{ill|Atami Station|en|Atami Station|qid=Q800412|lt=Atami Station|1=Atami Station}} is the first station on the {{ill|Itō Line|en|Itō Line|qid=Q1049129|lt=Itō Line|1=Itō Line}} and serves as a gateway to Izu tourism in the east. === Foot Baths and Hand Baths === * {{ill|Ie-yasu no Yu|ja|家康の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ie-yasu no Yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493085|1=Atami Iyeyasu hot spring}} – A free foot bath facility located in front of {{ill|Atami Station|en|Atami Station|qid=Q800412|lt=Atami Station|1=Atami Station}}. * {{ill|Fukufuku no Yu|ja|福福の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukufuku no Yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – A free hand bath facility located in the {{ill|Heiwadori Shopping Street|ja|平和通り商店街|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heiwadori Shopping Street|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. === Day-Use Bath Facilities (Excluding Communal Baths) === Numerous day-use bath facilities (including those integrated with hotels and ryokan) are available for external bathing.[http://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kanko/onsen/1001821.html Day-Use Bath Facilities｜Atami City Official Website] * {{ill|Fukushima-ya Ryokan|ja|福島屋旅館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushima-ya Ryokan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Nikkotei Ōyu|ja|日航亭大湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkotei Ōyu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Closed as of November 2023{{Cite web|和書|url=https://news.at-s.com/article/1560527 |title=Atami Nostalgia: A Public Bath Reopens After 35 Years, with Dining Renovations “A Place to Relax” |website= |publisher=Shizuoka Shimbun |accessdate=2024-09-22|qq=}}) * {{ill|Myōraku-yu|ja|妙楽湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōraku-yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Atami Onsen Yuyado Ichibanchi|ja|熱海温泉 湯宿一番地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Onsen Yuyado Ichibanchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ōedo Onsen Monogatari Atami|ja|大江戸温泉物語あたみ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōedo Onsen Monogatari Atami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Otsuki Hotel Wa-fūkan|ja|大月ホテル和風館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsuki Hotel Wa-fūkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [https://yumeiroha-atami.jp/] * {{ill|Hotel Mikurasu|ja|ホテルミクラス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotel Mikurasu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tsuki no Sukis Atami Gūraku Hotel|ja|月の栖熱海聚楽ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuki no Sukis Atami Gūraku Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Shūkakenyu no Hana-kazen|ja|秀花園湯の花膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkakenyu no Hana-kazen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ryori Ryokan Nagisa-kan|ja|料理旅館渚館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryori Ryokan Nagisa-kan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yasuragi no Yado Minoya|ja|やすらぎの宿みのや|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasuragi no Yado Minoya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|KKR Hotel Atami|ja|KKRホテル熱海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KKR Hotel Atami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Shinkadoya|ja|新かどや|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkadoya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ryokan Tachibana|ja|旅館立花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryokan Tachibana|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yuyado Mikan no Ki|ja|湯宿みかんの木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuyado Mikan no Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Umi no Hotel Nakadaya|ja|うみのホテル中田屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umi no Hotel Nakadaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Iso Ryōri Umi-be no Yado Hiratsuru|ja|磯料理 海辺の湯の宿 平鶴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iso Ryōri Umi-be no Yado Hiratsuru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Isobune|ja|磯舟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Izu Amishiro Onsen Taiseikan|ja|伊豆網代温泉 大成館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izu Amishiro Onsen Taiseikan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Chikurin-an Mizu no|ja|竹林庵みずの|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikurin-an Mizu no|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yuyado Ōkawa|ja|湯の宿おお川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuyado Ōkawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Heartpia Atami|ja|ハートピア熱海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heartpia Atami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502399|1=Heartopia Atami}} * {{ill|Rabista Izu-yama|ja|ラビスタ伊豆山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rabista Izu-yama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Aji to Yuno Yado 11 Tomiyoshi|ja|味と湯の宿ニューとみよし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji to Yuno Yado 11 Tomiyoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kanpo no Yado Atami Bekkan|ja|かんぽの宿 熱海別館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpo no Yado Atami Bekkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Shima no Yu|ja|島の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima no Yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135502402}} (Hatsushima) * {{ill|Ocean Spa Fuua|ja|オーシャン スパ Fuua|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ocean Spa Fuua|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493069|1=Ocean Spa Fuua}} (Attached to Atami Kōraku-en Hotel) * {{ill|Libmax Resort Atami Seafront|ja|リブマックスリゾート熱海シーフロント|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Libmax Resort Atami Seafront|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502404|1=Libmax Resort Atami Seafront}} * {{ill|Libmax Resort Atami Ocean|ja|リブマックスリゾート熱海オーシャン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Libmax Resort Atami Ocean|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502406|1=Libmax Resort Atami Ocean}} === Communal Baths === There are three communal baths available.[http://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kanko/onsen/1001821.html Day-Use Bath Facilities｜Atami City Official Website] Most of these are modest facilities that do not exude the lively atmosphere typical of Atami. * Operating: ** {{ill|Atami Ekimae Onsen Bathhouse (Tahara Bathhouse)|ja|熱海駅前温泉浴場（田原浴場）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Ekimae Onsen Bathhouse (Tahara Bathhouse)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502408|1=Atami Ekimae Onsen Bathhouse}} ** {{ill|Yamada-yu|ja|山田湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada-yu|1=Yamada-yu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q86730426}} ** {{ill|Take-no-sawa Communal Bath|ja|竹の沢共同浴場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-no-sawa Communal Bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502410|1=Take-no-sawa Communal Bath}} (Since 2005, converted into a members-only facility for local residents; external bathing has been discontinued.) * Closed: ** {{ill|Ujuku Shinjuku Communal Bath|ja|上宿新宿共同浴場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujuku Shinjuku Communal Bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502412|1=Ujuku Shinjuku Communal Bath}} ** {{ill|Nagisa Communal Bath|ja|渚共同浴場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagisa Communal Bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502415|1=Nagisa Communal Bath}} ** {{ill|Shimizu-chō Communal Bath|ja|清水町共同浴場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu-chō Communal Bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502418|1=Shimizu-chō Communal Bath}} ** {{ill|Mizuguchi Communal Bath|ja|水口共同浴場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuguchi Communal Bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502420|1=Mizuguchi Communal Bath}} ** {{ill|Mizuguchi No.2 Communal Bath|ja|水口第2共同浴場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuguchi No.2 Communal Bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502423|1=Mizuguchi No.2 Communal Bath}} === Atami Nanayu (Seven Onsen of Atami) === The hot spring town once featured seven natural sources, known as '''Atami Nanayu''' (熱海七湯), dating back to the Edo period; however, due to the impact of subsequent source development from the Meiji period onward, the natural discharge of these springs was lost. The source known as {{ill|Ōyu|ja|大湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu|1=Ōyu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11437542}} was restored as an artificial geyser in 1962 (Shōwa 37), while the other six were restored in 1997 (Heisei 9) as cultural and tourist resources by artificially inducing municipal hot spring discharge.[http://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kanko/onsen/1001823.html Atami Nanayu Tour] - Atami City The Seven Onsen of Atami are as follows: * '''Ōyu''' (大湯, also known as {{ill|Ōyu Geyser|ja|大湯間歇泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu Geyser|1=Ōyu Geyser|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65258433}}) – formerly the central source of Atami Onsen. * '''Nonaka no Yu''' (野中の湯) – located to the east of {{ill|Fujimori Inari Shrine|ja|藤森稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujimori Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502426|1=Fujimori Inari Shrine}}, with visible steam. * '''Kosawa no Yu''' (小沢の湯) – named after its locality; situated to the north behind the {{ill|Shizuoka Bank|en|Shizuoka Bank|qid=Q7499501|lt=Shizuoka Bank|1=Shizuoka Bank}} Atami branch along Onsen Street; its high-temperature steam is even used to boil eggs. A drinking fountain for water from the {{ill|Tanna Tunnel|ja|丹那湧水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanna Tunnel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is also provided. * '''Furo no Yu''' (風呂の湯) – located adjacent to {{ill|Fukushima-ya Ryokan|ja|福島屋旅館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushima-ya Ryokan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, usually paired with {{ill|Mizu no Yu|ja|水の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizu no Yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. * '''Me no Yu''' (目の湯) – named for its reputed healing properties; located along Ginza-dōri between {{ill|Suruga Bank|ja|スルガ銀行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suruga Bank|1=Suruga Bank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11313470}} and {{ill|Shizuoka Chūō Bank|ja|静岡中央銀行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shizuoka Chūō Bank|1=Shizuoka Chuo Bank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11662920}}; formerly also known as '''Sajirō no Yu''' (佐治郎の湯) after its former owner. * '''Seizaemon no Yu''' (清左右衛門の湯) – named after a deceased farmer; located west of {{ill|Furutani Ryokan|ja|古谷旅館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furutani Ryokan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with visible steam. * '''Kawahara Yu''' (河原湯) – formerly a source for the common people, located on a riverside at Higashihama; situated along {{ill|Japan National Route 135|en|Japan National Route 135|qid=Q6156779|lt=National Route 135|1=Japan National Route 135}} near the southern end of the Seven Onsen; note the nearby convenience store. File:230127 Oyu Atami Onsen Atami Japan01s3.jpg|Ōyu File:230127 Sajiro-no-yu Atami Onsen Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Sajirō no Yu (Me no Yu) File:230127 Ozawa-no-yu Atami Onsen Atami Japan03s3.jpg|Kosawa no Yu ==== Ōyu Geyser ==== {{main|Ōyu Geyser|qq=}} [[File:Geyser in Atami.jpg|thumb|Atami’s geyser in the Meiji era]] [[File:230127 Oyu Atami Onsen Atami Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Ōyu Geyser
(The current one is artificial)]] The {{ill|Ōyu Geyser|ja|大湯間歇泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu Geyser|1=Ōyu Geyser|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65258433}} was once reputed to be one of the world’s three major {{ill|Geyser|en|Geyser|qid=Q83471|lt=geyser|1=Geyser}}s, but due to extensive drilling in the surrounding area after the Meiji period, its natural discharge diminished and it no longer erupts spontaneously. It is now maintained as an artificial geyser. In the past, it erupted several times per day; records from the late Edo period counted up to eight eruptions per day, but its frequency decreased during the Meiji period.Tomohiko Takayanagi, ''Rekishi to Keizai'' 2006, Vol.48, Issue 3, pp.41–58, {{doi|10.20633/rekishitokeizai.48.3_41|qq=}} Conflicts arose between advocates for preserving Ōyu and factions seeking to expand the hot spring town beyond it. In 1883, records indicate that eruptions occurred three times during both day and night. Around 1902 (mid-Meiji period), spontaneous eruptions ceased. Alarmed, local residents commissioned physicist [[Honda Kōtarō|Honda Kōtarō]] who, with his assistant [[:en:Terada Torahiko|Terada Torahiko]], succeeded in restoring the geyser.Atsushi Katsuki, [https://hdl.handle.net/2433/89730 Honda’s Magnetic Theory and the Acceptance Process of Weiss Theory in Japan: Based on Oral Accounts] ''Bussō Kenkyū'' 1978, Vol.31, Issue 1, pp.1– (Bussō Kenkyūkai) At that time, the geyser erupted on average four times per day for about eight minutes each.[[Kozu Sotsuyū]], [https://doi.org/10.5026/jgeography.24.754 Atami Onsen Survey Report] ''Chigaku Zasshi'' 1912, Vol.24, Issue 11, pp.754–764, {{doi|10.5026/jgeography.24.754|qq=}} Later, in Taishō 12, spontaneous eruptions ceased and the source became depleted, prompting the lifting of drilling restrictions and a surge in drilling on the mountainside and reclaimed coastal areas; by Shōwa 10, as many as 220 wells had been drilled. However, new drilling was later suspended; during a 1912 survey by [[Kozu Sotsuyū|Kozu Sotsuyū]], the average eruption had declined to about two eruptions per day, each lasting three minutes. After the Great Kantō Earthquake, spontaneous eruptions resumed briefly but ceased again in the early Shōwa period. Finally, in 1962 (Shōwa 37), the municipal hot spring system was used to pump water, and an artificial geyser was established, which remains in operation today. A replica of Japan’s first {{ill|Payphone|en|Payphone|qid=Q900977|lt=public telephone|1=Payphone}} box has been installed near the annex of the {{ill|Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel|ja|熱海ニューフジヤホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel|1=Atami New Fujiya Hotel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11569074}}. Additionally, a monument commemorates [[Rutherford Allcock]], the first recorded British envoy to reach the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} during the Bakumatsu period, and the grave of his Scottish Terrier Toby, who accompanied him, suffered severe burns at this geyser on his return journey, and later died there. Although Toby received extensive care from local residents, he ultimately passed away. The grave was erected by local residents in mourning, and legend has it that Allcock expressed his gratitude; the tombstone bears the inscription “poor Toby (かわいそうなトビー)”. == == History == [[File:Zushu_atami_zenzu.jpg|alt=Full map of Atami Onsen from the Meiji era|thumb]] [[File:Atami_Onsen_in_Taisho_era.jpg|alt=Taishō era|thumb]] [[File:A_party_of_Japanese_visitors_at_the_hot_springs_resort_of_Atami,_near_Tokyo.jpg|alt=Shōwa era|thumb]] [[File:View_of_Atami.JPG|alt=Year 2011|thumb]] Atami is historically an ancient hot spring resort. It is said that about 1500 years ago, during the time of {{ill|Emperor Ninken|en|Emperor Ninken|qid=Q357266|lt=Emperor Ninken|1=Emperor Ninken}}, boiling hot water gushed forth from the sea, causing fish to rot and die—a phenomenon discovered by local residents. Because of this, the place was named Atami, meaning “hot sea.” Furthermore, there is a legend that during the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|qid=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō Hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} period, {{ill|Mankai Jōnin|ja|万巻上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankai Jōnin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 万巻|1=Mangan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11353818}} of {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Gongen|qq=}}, in order to relieve fishermen suffering from poor catches caused by this “hot sea,” prayed and transferred the source from the sea to its present location in the mountain village.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.city.atami.shizuoka.jp/page.php?p_id=1100|title=熱海の温泉|publisher=熱海市役所|accessdate=2016-04-10|qq=}} During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, in {{ill|Ōan|en|Ōan|qid=Q1146245|lt=Ōan era|1=Ōan}}7/{{ill|Bunchū|en|Bunchū|qid=Q1194181|lt=Bunchū era|1=Bunchū}}3 ({{ill|1374|en|1374|qid=Q6269|lt=1374 AD|1=1374}}), on March 4, a Kamakura native, {{ill|Gidō Shūshin|en|Gidō Shūshin|qid=Q1335613|lt=Gidō Shūshin|1=Gidō Shūshin}}, visited Atami Onsen. He noted in his diary that a poetry gathering had been held on the occasion of the {{ill|Double Third Festival|en|Double Third Festival|qid=Q698091|lt=Shōmatsuri Festival (March 3)|1=Double Third Festival}}, at which he composed {{ill|Shi (poetry)|en|Shi (poetry)|qid=Q1759242|lt=Chinese poetry|1=Shi (poetry)}} (as recorded in the ''{{ill|Kūka Nichiyō Kōfuryaku-shū|ja|空華日用工夫略集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūka Nichiyō Kōfuryaku-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', entry for Ōan 7, March 4). This record not only indicates that many literati frequented Atami at the time but also is known as an anecdote concerning the discrepancy between the dates of the {{ill|Kyō calendar|ja|京暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyō calendar|qq=}} used in Kyoto and the {{ill|Mishima calendar|ja|三島暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishima calendar|1=Mishima calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11355249}} used in Atami.桃裕行「京暦と三島暦との日の食違いについて」『桃裕行著作集』第8巻、詩文閣出版、1990th year 、P223-230.（原論文:『天文総報』第14巻4号、1960th year 4月） In the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, in the 9th year of the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō era|1=Keichō}} (1604), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} stayed for seven days for {{ill|yūji|ja|湯治|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yūji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1004887}} (hot spring treatment), as recorded in the ''{{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2439031}}''. Since then, as a famed hot spring favored by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|徳川将軍家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川宗家}} and its {{ill|Royal warrant of appointment|en|Royal warrant of appointment|qid=Q1070771|lt=Officially Patronized|1=Royal warrant of appointment}}, from the time of {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|qid=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} onward, Atami’s hot spring water was presented to Edo Castle through the "Gokyu-yu" ceremony.{{Cite book|和書 |title=熱海錦嚢 |year=1897|publisher=芹沢政吉 |author=斎藤要人 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/764830/1/32|qq=}} After the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} period, many literati visited Atami, and numerous works were set in this locale. The most representative work is {{ill|The Golden Demon|ja|金色夜叉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Golden Demon|1=Konjiki Yasha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11647825}} by {{ill|Ozaki Kōyō|en|Ozaki Kōyō|qid=Q975273|lt=Ozaki Kōyō|1=Ozaki Kōyō}}, through which Atami became known nationwide. Along {{ill|Japan National Route 135|en|Japan National Route 135|qid=Q6156779|lt=National Route 135|1=Japan National Route 135}}, statues of the characters Kan'ichi and Omiya can be found. Other notable works include {{ill|Winter Day|ja|冬の日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Day|1=Winter Day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11395202}} by {{ill|Kafū Nagai|en|Kafū Nagai|qid=Q530588|lt=Nagai Kafū|1=Kafū Nagai}} and {{ill|Uzu Shio|ja|うず潮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uzu Shio|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to うずしお|1=Uzushio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16874528}} by {{ill|Fumiko Hayashi (author)|en|Fumiko Hayashi (author)|qid=Q445839|lt=Hayashi Fumiko|1=Fumiko Hayashi (author)}}. In 1923 (Taishō 12), Atami suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3742341}}, but with the extension of Atami Station on the {{ill|Atami Line|ja|熱海線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Line|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東海道本線|1=Tōkaidō Main Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1190152}} of the national railway the following year, the area quickly recovered; on July 1, 1936 (Shōwa 11), the Atami Town-operated Onsen (later City-operated Onsen) was established.[https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kurashi/suido/1004882/1005368.html "市営温泉のはじまり] At that time, Atami was such a thriving hot spring resort that newspapers described it as "a town of eroticism, a town of {{ill|Cafe|ja|カフェー (風俗営業)|lt=Cafe|lt_ja=カフェー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cafe|1=jokyū café|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11294599}}," reflecting the booming {{ill|sex industry|ja|風俗産業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sex industry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 風俗|1=Mores|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1115221}}. However, in {{ill|1934|en|1934|qid=Q18714|lt=1934 AD|1=1934}} (Shōwa 9), when the {{ill|Tanna Tunnel|en|Tanna Tunnel|qid=Q7683603|lt=Tanna Tunnel|1=Tanna Tunnel}} opened and tourists flocked to the hot springs, many cafes were converted into dining establishments.開通後初の正月に伊豆の温泉は超満員『東京日日新聞』昭和10th year 1月8日（『昭和ニュース事典第5巻 昭和10th year -昭和11th year 』本編p363 昭和ニュース事典編纂委員会 毎日コミュニケーションズ刊 1994th year ) In the postwar period of turmoil, a {{ill|Panpan district|ja|パンパン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Panpan district|1=Panpan girls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11329737}} emerged, characterized by a decline in public morals and catering to occupation army soldiers, among others.[https://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/001095/files/43178_21390.html]{{ill|Aozora Bunko|en|Aozora Bunko|qid=Q83466|lt=Aozora Bunko|1=Aozora Bunko}} Subsequently, Atami suffered great damage in the {{ill|Atami Great Fire|ja|熱海大火|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Great Fire|1=Atami Great Fire|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131919958}}, but during the Shōwa 30s it became a prime destination for honeymoon trips, bustling with couples ({{ill|couple|ja|カップル|lt=Abecc|lt_ja=アベック|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/couple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Couple (relationship)|qid=Q219160}} recognizable by their white dresses and white suitcases). After the opening of the {{ill|Tokaido Shinkansen|en|Tokaido Shinkansen|qid=Q660895|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|1=Tokaido Shinkansen}}, in the Shōwa 40s, the impact of the {{ill|Japanese economic miracle|en|Japanese economic miracle|qid=Q1059474|lt=High Economic Growth Period|1=Japanese economic miracle}} led to a focus on attracting {{ill|group tours|ja|団体旅行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/group tours|1=group tours|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420283}}, and visitors from not only the Kantō region but also from the {{ill|Chūkyō metropolitan area|en|Chūkyō metropolitan area|qid=Q1377209|lt=Chukyo|1=Chūkyō metropolitan area}} and {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai|1=Kansai region}} increased, with the number of overnight guests exceeding 4.5 million annually.[https://www.sankei.com/article/20180827-FO6IEV7LEFO7BIFPCMMRG5DI34/ “Atami Revival: A Successful Community-wide Effort in Town Revitalization” {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|en|Sankei Shimbun|qid=Q1197261|lt=Sankei Shimbun|1=Sankei Shimbun}}, morning edition, September 2, 2018 (comprehensive), viewed September 19, 2018] During the bubble era, soaring land prices in the {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|en|Special wards of Tokyo|qid=Q308891|lt=Tokyo metropolitan area|1=Special wards of Tokyo}} and the spread of {{ill|Shinkansen commuting|ja|新幹線通勤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkansen commuting|1=Shinkansen commuting|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11502167}} led to many high-income individuals building homes with hot springs in Atami and commuting to Tokyo via the Shinkansen. However, due to the {{ill|Lost Decades|en|Lost Decades|qid=Q1141122|lt=bubble collapse|1=Lost Decades}}, corporate resort facilities and group tours declined, and with the generalization of {{ill|overseas travel|ja|海外旅行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/overseas travel|1=international tourism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q120334380}} amid a {{ill|yen appreciation|ja|円高|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yen appreciation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円相場|1=Japanese Yen rate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11394831}}, the number of visitors from distant regions—including from Kansai—dropped. Moreover, a series of earthquakes off the coast of Ito in the early 1990s further compounded the decline in tourism, and in 2006, Atami City even declared a fiscal crisis.[https://www.travelvoice.jp/20201108-147348 衰退から一転、「熱海の奇跡」が実現した舞台裏を、再生キーパーソンに聞いてきた　―街づくりと観光の連動から「関係人口」の創出まで｜トラベルボイス（観光産業ニュース）] In response, local volunteers began to take action.[https://gendai.media/articles/-/56405 衰退一途の熱海がいつのまにか復活を遂げた「再生の秘密」（市来 広一郎） | 現代ビジネス | 講談社（1/4） ] In the Atami Ginza Shopping Street, which connects Atami Station with the ryokan and hotel district, vacant storefronts that had proliferated were revitalized through efforts by {{ill|U-turn|en|U-turn|qid=Q1343116|lt=U-turn|1=U-turn}} returnees from Tokyo, local residents, and the city’s initiative to hold open-air markets and attract enterprising shop owners through business model transformations. Thus, the long-term downward trend bottomed out in 2011 and reversed, drawing attention as the “Atami Revival.” The inherent advantage of being located adjacent to a Shinkansen station in the western part of the {{ill|Greater Tokyo Area|en|Greater Tokyo Area|qid=Q328121|lt=Metropolitan Area|1=Greater Tokyo Area}} remains intact, and the ryokan and hotel industries have succeeded by enhancing their appeal to individual and small-group travelers through events such as {{ill|firework|ja|花火大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fireworks festivals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 花火|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q127933|lt=fireworks|1=Fireworks}}.[https://president.jp/articles/-/23322 なぜ「熱海」は人気観光地に返り咲いたか アラフィフのおじさんたちが大奮闘] {{ill|President Online|ja|プレジデント社|lt=President Online|lt_ja=プレジデント・オンライン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/President Online|1=President|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11335839}} (October 12, 2017) viewed September 19, 2018] === History of Hotels === * November 30, 1976 (Shōwa 51): A fire broke out at the {{ill|Atami Rinkai Hotel|ja|熱海臨海ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Rinkai Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502429|1=Atami Rinkai Hotel}}, resulting in 2 casualties. * Around 1990 (Heisei 2): Following the {{ill|Lost Decades|en|Lost Decades|qid=Q1141122|lt=bubble collapse|1=Lost Decades}}, group tourism declined significantly. Many large ryokan that did not target individual customers closed in succession, giving the town a deserted impression and further accelerating the loss of guests. * August 30, 1999 (Heisei 11): SF Shōji, which operated both the {{ill|Atami Fujiya Hotel|ja|熱海富士屋ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Fujiya Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502431|1=Atami Fujiya Hotel}} and the {{ill|Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel|ja|熱海ニュー富士屋ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502433|1=Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel}}, applied for corporate rehabilitation with debts amounting to 99 billion yen. * October 1, 1999 (Heisei 11): {{ill|Dankaisō|ja|暖海荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dankaisō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} filed for bankruptcy with debts of 126 billion yen. * 2000 (Heisei 12): The operator of {{ill|Kiyunkaku|ja|起雲閣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyunkaku|1=Kiunkaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11636134}}, {{ill|Japan Tourism Co., Ltd.|ja|日本観光株式会社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Tourism Co., Ltd.|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, went bankrupt with debts of 192 billion yen. * 2001 (Heisei 13): The long-established, large ryokan {{ill|Tsuruya Hotel|ja|つるやホテル (熱海温泉)|lt=Tsuruya Hotel|lt_ja=つるやホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruya Hotel|1=Tsuruya Hotel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60987069}} ceased operations. * 2004 (Heisei 16): {{ill|Atami Kinjōkan|ja|熱海金城館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Kinjōkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} filed for bankruptcy with debts of 31 billion yen. * 2006 (Heisei 18): The venerable, large hotel {{ill|Ōtsuki Hotel|ja|大月ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsuki Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135502435}} applied for corporate rehabilitation with debts of 46 billion yen. At that time, facilities closed in the following order—Ōtsuki Hotel, Tsuruya Hotel, Dankaisō, {{ill|Atami Grand Hotel|ja|熱海グランドホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Grand Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502437|1=Atami Grand Hotel}}, {{ill|Hotel 11 Yamato|ja|ホテル ニュー大和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotel 11 Yamato|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502440|1=Hotel 11 Yamato}}, and {{ill|Atami Rinkai Hotel|ja|熱海臨海ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Rinkai Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502429|1=Atami Rinkai Hotel}}—resulting in an eerie landscape of abandoned ruins. * May 15, 2008 (Heisei 20): {{ill|Womizaki Hotel|ja|ウオミサキホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womizaki Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502442|1=Womizaki Hotel}} filed for personal bankruptcy at the Numazu branch of the Shizuoka District Court with debts of 23.5 billion yen. * May 2010 (Heisei 22): The operator of {{ill|Okamoto Hotel|ja|岡本ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okamoto Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502444|1=Okamoto Hotel}}, {{ill|Okamoto Club|ja|岡本倶楽部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okamoto Club|1=Okamoto Club|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55535752}}, went bankrupt. Later, those involved were punished for violations of the {{ill|Organized Crime Punishment Act|ja|組織的な犯罪の処罰及び犯罪収益の規制等に関する法律|lt=Organized Crime Punishment Act|lt_ja=組織犯罪処罰法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Organized Crime Punishment Act|1=Act on Punishment of Organized Crimes and Control of Proceeds of Crime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11606344}}.{{Cite web|和書|date=2013-05-30|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG30014_Q3A530C1CC0000/|title=岡本ホテル事件、実質オーナーに懲役18th year 　東京地裁判決|publisher=日本経済新聞|accessdate=2018-06-22|qq=}} * November 19, 2010 (Heisei 22): {{ill|Ōnoya|ja|大野屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnoya|1=Ōnoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11439581}} applied for corporate rehabilitation at the Numazu branch of the Shizuoka District Court with debts of 21.5 billion yen. * February 25, 2011 (Heisei 23): The employee dormitory of {{ill|Shihomiya Ryokan|ja|志ほみや旅館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihomiya Ryokan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502446|1=Shihomiya Ryokan}} was completely destroyed by fire; fortunately, proper evacuation prevented any injuries. * October 15, 2011 (Heisei 23): {{ill|Nanmei Hotel|ja|南明ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanmei Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502449|1=Nanmei Hotel}} filed for personal bankruptcy at the Numazu branch of the Shizuoka District Court with debts of 17 billion yen. * July 28, 2018 (Heisei 30): Due to a storm surge from Typhoon No. 12, the restaurant windows of {{ill|Hotel 11 Akao|ja|ホテルニューアカオ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotel 11 Akao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ACAO SPA & RESORT|1=Hotel New Akao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q63801636}} were broken. * July 3, 2021 (Reiwa 3): {{ill|Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel|ja|熱海ニュー富士屋ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135502433|1=Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel}} and {{ill|Hotel 11 Akao|ja|ホテルニューアカオ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotel 11 Akao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ACAO SPA & RESORT|1=Hotel New Akao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q63801636}} served as evacuation shelters for victims of the {{ill|2021 Atami landslide|en|2021 Atami landslide|qid=Q107405872|lt=Atami City Izu Mountain Debris Flow Disaster|1=2021 Atami landslide}}. == Byword for Entertainment Districts == Because Atami Onsen once flourished as Japan’s representative entertainment hot spring, similar or famous {{ill|Spa town|en|Spa town|qid=Q4946461|lt=Onsen town|1=Spa town}}s and {{ill|Hot Spring Resort|ja|温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Resort|1=onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11562971}}s in other regions have been advertised as “the Atami of ○○” or referred to as such. Similarly, these hot spring towns, like Atami Onsen, have experienced a decline in visitors due to factors such as the collapse of the bubble economy and the diversification of {{ill|Leisure|en|Leisure|qid=Q180910|lt=Leisure|1=Leisure}}, and there are now instances where existing hot spring towns are described in contexts such as “the XX Onsen that was once called the Atami of ○○ …”{{cite news|url=http://www.mutusinpou.co.jp/%E6%B4%A5%E8%BB%BD%E3%81%AE%E8%A1%97%E3%81%A8%E9%A2%A8%E6%99%AF/2015/03/35640.html|title=Asamushi Onsen Becomes an Entertainment District|newspaper={{ill|Mutsu Shinpo|ja|陸奥新報|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsu Shinpo|1=Mutsu Shinpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11658598}}|publisher={{ill|Mutsu Shinpo|ja|陸奥新報社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsu Shinpo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陸奥新報|1=Mutsu Shinpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11658598}}|date=2015-3-23|author=Miho Nakazono (Researcher, Aomori Prefectural History Compilation)|accessdate=2015-11-28|qq=}}Example of Asamushi Onsen being called “the Atami of Tōhoku” – ‘{{ill|Kahoku Shimpō|en|Kahoku Shimpō|qid=Q639774|lt=Kahoku Shimpo|1=Kahoku Shimpō}}’ August 11, 1994. On the other hand, there are cases such as {{ill|Kaike Onsen|ja|皆生温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaike Onsen|1=Kaike Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11580978}}, which brands itself as “the Atami of the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|qid=Q398310|lt=San'in|1=San'in region}},” using the term as a straightforward promotional slogan without any negative or passive connotations. In a special case, {{ill|Bandai Atami Onsen|ja|磐梯熱海温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Atami Onsen|1=Bandai Atami Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11587893}} was so named because the lord at the time of its opening was originally from Izu, and thus it has become a name closely associated with the local area. == Closed Facilities ==
Closed Facilities
* Atami Grand Hotel Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City Closed in 1995. It had become a ruin, but was demolished in 2003. * Hotel Ichifuji 9-2, Sakimachi, Atami City Demolished around 1996, and a condominium "Atami City Villa Sakimachi" was constructed in its place. * Hotel Atamikaku 4-16, Taharamoto-cho, Atami City Currently in operation under the management of Itoen Hotel. * Hotel Dankaisou 12, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City Declared bankrupt in 1999 with debts of 12 billion yen. It was demolished. The land was auctioned, and although the Manyo Group planned to purchase it and construct a hotel, the plan was scrapped. * Isobenkai 3-8, Sakimachi, Atami City It has become known as "Sophia Atami Deisas." * Kiunkaku 4-2, Shōwa-cho, Atami City In 1999, its operating company, Japan Kankō, went bankrupt with debts of 19.2 billion yen. Subsequently, it came under the management of Atami City and is open to the public. * Shōtō Main Building 5-30, Wadahama Minamimachi, Atami City Closed in 1999; later became a ruin and was demolished in 2016. * Atami 11 Fujiya Hotel 1-16, Ginza-cho, Atami City In 1999, its operating company, SF Shōji, went bankrupt with debts of 9.9 billion yen. In 2006, it was acquired by Itoen Hotel and resumed operations. * Atami Fujiya Hotel 13-18, Ginza-cho, Atami City In 1999, its operating company, SF Shōji, went bankrupt with debts of 9.9 billion yen, leading to its closure and demolition. A condominium, "Lebens Resort Atami," was constructed in its place. * Atami Rinkai Hotel 11-24, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City On November 30, 1976, a fire occurred causing fatal burns. It went bankrupt in 2000. Around 2007, it was demolished and became the condominium "Zephyr Atami Beach Tower." * Atami Onsen Hotel 4-6, Sakimachi Formerly a rest facility for Tokyo Metropolitan Government employees, it closed at the end of May 2001. It was later renovated and reopened as "Hotel Aiola," but then closed again. It is currently operating as "Yumeiroha." * Atami Kankō Hotel 2-11, Taharamoto-cho, Atami City Closed at the end of June 2001. It was demolished, and a condominium "Leaage Atami" was built in its place. * Tsuruya Hotel 6-45, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City Closed at the end of November 2001. It was demolished around 2005, and a multi-purpose building "Aune Atami" was constructed but went bankrupt during construction. After being left abandoned for 10 years, it reopened in 2020 as the "Atami Pearl Star Hotel" under foreign capital. * Isobenkai 2-13, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City Ceased operations in December 2001. It was demolished and became the condominium "Duopia Atami." * Atami 11 Asahi 7-10, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City Bankrupted around 2002. It was demolished around 2006, and in 2012, a members-only hotel "Tokyu Vacations Atami" was constructed. * Atami Kinjōkan 10-33, Shōwa-cho, Atami City Declared bankrupt in November 2004 with debts of 3.1 billion yen. Subsequently, it became part of the Itoen Hotel group and resumed operations. * Ōtsuki Hotel (First Generation) 3-19, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City At the end of May 2006, it went bankrupt with debts of 4.6 billion yen. Later, with capital participation from Orix, it resumed operations. * Nishi-Atami Hotel 1-1, Umezono-cho, Atami City A hotel operated by Izuhakone Railway that closed at the end of August 2006. It was demolished around 2010 and became a condominium. * Kokumin Shukusha Atami Village 21-7, Umezono-cho, Atami City Closed on January 15, 2008. It is currently operated by Daiichi Kōshō Co., Ltd. as the lodging facility "Utayuno Yado Atami Shiki Hotel." * Womizaki Hotel 7-2, Wadahama Minamimachi, Atami City Declared bankrupt in May 2008 with debts of 2.35 billion yen. Later that same year, it was acquired by Itoen Hotel and resumed operations. * Atami Okamoto Hotel 1, Kamiyado-cho, Atami City Closed in 2010 due to the bankruptcy of the Okamoto Group. It was later demolished, and a public facility is planned to be built on the site. * Atami Hotel Kaizan 11-19, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City Bankrupted and closed in 2010. Currently, the new building is used as a camp facility for group driving license training by a driving school. * Hotel Ōnoya 3-9, Wadahama Minamimachi, Atami City Declared bankrupt in November 2010 with debts of 2.15 billion yen. Subsequently, it came under the umbrella of Itoen Hotel and resumed operations. * Nanmei Hotel 8-3, Sakimachi, Atami City Declared bankrupt in November 2011 with debts of 1.7 billion yen. Afterwards, Ōedo Onsen Monogatari acquired the hotel and reopened it as "Ōedo Onsen Monogatari Atami." * Hotel South Bay 6-49, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City Originally an annex of Nanmei Hotel, it ceased operations in 2011 following Nanmei Hotel's bankruptcy and has since been left abandoned. * Shiroki Ryokan 7-6, Nagisacho, Atami City Ceased operations at the end of January 2012, was demolished, and later became a condominium. * Hotel Ikeda 12-40, Higashi-kaigan-cho, Atami City Ceased operations on January 21, 2012, and was demolished. * SS Seiyaku Atami Retreat (Closed around 1990) Later became a retreat facility for Jonan Shinyokinko, but closed again around 2014. Since 2015, it has been operating as "Atami Fuga."
== Related Topics == {{Wiki Portal Link|Hot Springs|[[File:Hot springs 001.svg|34px|Portal:Hot Springs]]|qq=}} * {{ill|Japan's Twenty-Five Victories|ja|日本二十五勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Twenty-Five Victories|1=Japan's Twenty-Five Victories|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q21654073}} * {{ill|Hihōkan|ja|秘宝館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hihōkan|1=erotic museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1191610}} * {{ill|Tokyo Story|en|Tokyo Story|qid=Q26060|lt=Tokyo Story|1=Tokyo Story}} – The scenery of {{ill|Atami Castle|en|Atami Castle|qid=Q11257168|lt=Atami Castle|1=Atami Castle}} and the pre-hotel era of {{ill|Nishikigaura|ja|錦ヶ浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikigaura|1=Nishikigaura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11650415}} can be glimpsed. * {{ill|Atami Hotel|ja|熱海ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atami Hotel|1=Atami Hotel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60987155}} == アクセス == * 鉄道 : JR[[:en:東海道新幹線|東海道新幹線]]またはJR{{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|qid=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}}[[熱海駅]]下車すぐ。海岸沿いの中心部へは、[[伊豆東海バス]]運行の[[路線バス]]で約5分。また、JR[[伊東線]][[来宮駅]]も有用。 * 自家用車：[[西湘バイパス]][[石橋インターチェンジ]]より[[国道135号]]経由。 == テレビ番組 == * [[日経スペシャル ガイアの夜明け]] よみがえれ！温泉街　～老舗の熱海・地震が襲った能登～（2007th year 5月29日、テレビ東京）[https://www.tv-tokyo.co.jp/gaia/backnumber/preview070529.html よみがえれ！温泉街　～老舗の熱海・地震が襲った能登～] - テレビ東京 2007th year 5月29日。- 温泉街再生に向けた試行錯誤の取組を取材。 == References == {{Reflist|25em|qq=}} == External links == * [https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kanko/onsen/1001820.html 熱海の温泉]（[https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/_res/projects/default_project/_page_/001/001/821/860item_file.pdf PDF]） - 熱海市 * [https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kanko/onsen/1001823.html 熱海七湯めぐり] - 熱海市 * [https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/_res/projects/default_project/_page_/001/001/821/856item_file.pdf 温泉万歳] - 熱海市観光課 * [https://www.ataminews.gr.jp/ 熱海市観光協会] {{Normdaten|qq=}} {{Onsen|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|熱海温泉|version=103415446|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[doi:10.20633/rekishitokeizai.48.3_41|Utilization of Sources in Hot Spring Areas: A Case Study of Prewar Atami Onsen]] [[ja:熱海温泉]] [[ja:静岡県]] [[ja:白浜温泉]] [[ja:和歌山県]] [[ja:小浜温泉]] [[ja:長崎県]] [[ja:瀬波温泉]] [[ja:新潟県]] [[ja:鹿児島県]] [[ja:指宿温泉]] [[ja:飯坂温泉]] [[ja:玉造温泉]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[zh:%E7%86%B1%E6%B5%B7%E6%BA%AB%E6%B3%89]] [[ko:%EC%95%84%ED%83%80%EB%AF%B8 %EC%98%A8%EC%B2%9C]] [[ja:%E7%86%B1%E6%B5%B7%E6%B8%A9%E6%B3%89]] [[en:Atami Onsen]] [[ko:아타미 온천]] [[zh:熱海溫泉]] [[en:Atami Onsen]] [[ko:아타미 온천]] [[zh:熱海溫泉]] {{wikidata link|Q11569090}} [[Category:Regional collective trademarks]] [[Category:熱海市の地理]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:Hot springs of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 100+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 150+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 200+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 250+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:incorrect template category applied]] [[Category:Pages with deleted QID in ill template]]
